title: Image Metadata & EXIF Forensics
description: Extract hidden data from photos — GPS locations, timestamps, camera info, and tampering evidence
learning_outcomes: Understand what EXIF metadata is and why it matters in forensic investigations

Extract EXIF data from JPEG images using Python libraries

The filling is the EXIF data (camera, GPS, timestamp — a structured data block). The bread on the bottom is the actual pixel data (what you see). Forensics examines ALL layers, not just the visible picture.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Open image in binary mode. Step 2: Check magic bytes (FF D8 FF = JPEG). Step 3: Parse the EXIF segment (starts after the header). Step 4: Extract tags by number (271=Make, 272=Model, 306=DateTime). Step 5: Convert GPS from degrees/minutes/seconds to decimal. Step 6: Report findings!

Each tag has a numeric ID mapped to a human-readable name.
explanation_styles.comparison: ExifTool (command line): exiftool photo.jpg — shows everything. Pillow (Python): img._getexif() — gives raw tag numbers. piexif (Python): piexif.load('photo.jpg') — gives structured EXIF dict. All read the same data, different interfaces.

lessons.title: Image Metadata & EXIF Forensics — Hidden Data in Every Photo

theory.introduction:
On September 2012, anti-virus pioneer John McAfee was on the run from Belizean authorities. A Vice Magazine journalist found him and took a photo. They posted it online — and forgot to strip the EXIF metadata. Within hours, people extracted the GPS coordinates from the image, revealing McAfee was hiding in Guatemala. He was arrested days later.

This is the power of image metadata forensics. Every digital photo contains HIDDEN DATA embedded inside the file — invisible when you look at the picture, but packed with information: the exact GPS coordinates where it was taken, the date and time (to the second), the camera make and model, the software used to edit it, lens settings, orientation, and sometimes even a thumbnail of the original uncropped image.

This hidden data is called EXIF — Exchangeable Image File Format. It was designed so cameras could store shooting settings (shutter speed, aperture, ISO). But modern smartphones add GPS coordinates, altitude, compass direction, and more. Most people have no idea their photos contain this information.

For forensic investigators, EXIF is a goldmine: you can prove WHERE and WHEN a photo was taken, WHICH device took it, whether it was EDITED, and even build TIMELINES across hundreds of photos. For privacy, it is a nightmare — every photo you share reveals your location history.

In this topic, you will learn to extract, analyse, and interpret image metadata using Python. You will build tools that do what professional forensic software does — and understand both the investigative power and privacy implications.

VM TIP: On your Kali Linux VM, install ExifTool: sudo apt install libimage-exiftool-perl. Then try: exiftool photo.jpg — it shows ALL metadata. Your Python scripts will do the same thing!
theory.why_important: Image metadata forensics has solved real crimes: kidnapping cases (GPS from ransom photos), fraud (timestamp proving a document was backdated), stalking (tracking someone through their photo locations), intellectual property theft (proving when an image was really created), and child exploitation cases (device identification linking photos to a specific camera). On the defensive side, understanding EXIF helps organisations strip sensitive metadata before publishing images, protecting employee and customer privacy. Social media platforms strip EXIF on upload, but the originals on devices retain everything. Law enforcement can subpoena the original files and extract the full metadata trail.

key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF stands for Exchangeable Image File Format. It is a standard that defines how metadata is stored INSIDE image files — primarily JPEG and TIFF formats. When your camera or phone takes a photo, it automatically writes dozens of data fields into the file: camera make and model, date and time, shutter speed, aperture, ISO, focal length, GPS coordinates, altitude, orientation (portrait/landscape), flash status, software used, and more.

This data lives INSIDE the image file itself — not in a separate file, not in the filename. You cannot see it by looking at the picture. You need special tools (or Python!) to read it.

PNG and GIF files do NOT use EXIF — they have simpler metadata formats. Screenshots typically have minimal metadata. JPEG is the primary format for EXIF forensics.
key_concepts.example:
# EXIF data structure (simplified)
exif_data = {
    'Make': 'Apple',              # Camera manufacturer
    'Model': 'iPhone 15 Pro',     # Camera model
    'DateTime': '2025:02:28 14:30:22',  # When taken
    'GPSLatitude': 51.5074,       # Where taken (lat)
    'GPSLongitude': -0.1278,      # Where taken (lon)
    'Software': 'Photos 9.0',     # Editing software
    'Orientation': 1,             # How to display
    'ExposureTime': '1/120',      # Shutter speed
    'FNumber': 1.78,              # Aperture
    'ISOSpeedRatings': 100,       # Light sensitivity
}

for key, value in exif_data.items():
    print(f"  {key:<20} {value}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: EXIF = hidden data INSIDE the image file. Camera, location, time, settings — all recorded automatically without your knowledge!

key_concepts.explanation:
Every image format has a unique file signature (magic bytes) at the very beginning. This lets forensic tools identify the TRUE file type regardless of the extension.

JPEG: FF D8 FF (first 3 bytes). PNG: 89 50 4E 47 (\x89PNG). GIF: 47 49 46 (GIF). BMP: 42 4D (BM). TIFF: 49 49 2A 00 (little-endian) or 4D 4D 00 2A (big-endian).

After the magic bytes, JPEG files contain SEGMENTS. The EXIF data lives in the APP1 segment, identified by marker FF E1. This segment contains a TIFF-structured block with all the metadata tags.
key_concepts.example:
# Image magic bytes identification
IMAGE_SIGNATURES = {
    b'\xff\xd8\xff': 'JPEG',
    b'\x89PNG': 'PNG',
    b'GIF87a': 'GIF87',
    b'GIF89a': 'GIF89a',
    b'BM': 'BMP',
    b'II*\x00': 'TIFF (little-endian)',
    b'MM\x00*': 'TIFF (big-endian)',
    b'RIFF': 'WEBP (possibly)',
}

def identify_image(filepath):
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        header = f.read(8)
    for sig, name in IMAGE_SIGNATURES.items():
        if header[:len(sig)] == sig:
            return name
    return f'Unknown: {header[:4].hex()}'
key_concepts.ai_hint: JPEG starts with FF D8 FF. PNG starts with 89 50 4E 47. These bytes CANNOT lie — renaming a .exe to .jpg does not change the magic bytes!

key_concepts.concept: EXIF Tag Numbers — The Dictionary of Metadata
key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF stores data as numbered TAGS. Each tag has an ID number and a value. Common tags:

Tag 271 = Make (camera manufacturer). Tag 272 = Model. Tag 306 = DateTime. Tag 274 = Orientation. Tag 305 = Software. Tag 33434 = ExposureTime. Tag 33437 = FNumber (aperture). Tag 34855 = ISOSpeedRatings.

GPS data lives in a separate IFD (Image File Directory) with its own tags: GPSTag 1 = LatitudeRef (N/S). GPSTag 2 = Latitude (degrees, minutes, seconds). GPSTag 3 = LongitudeRef (E/W). GPSTag 4 = Longitude. GPSTag 6 = Altitude.

Python's Pillow library uses the ExifTags module to convert these numbers to human-readable names.
key_concepts.example:
# EXIF tag number reference
COMMON_TAGS = {
    271: 'Make',           # Camera manufacturer
    272: 'Model',          # Camera model
    274: 'Orientation',    # Image rotation
    305: 'Software',       # Editing software
    306: 'DateTime',       # Date/time taken
    33434: 'ExposureTime', # Shutter speed
    33437: 'FNumber',      # Aperture
    34855: 'ISOSpeedRatings',
    36867: 'DateTimeOriginal',  # ORIGINAL capture time
    36868: 'DateTimeDigitized', # When digitised
    34853: 'GPSInfo',      # GPS data block
}

GPS_TAGS = {
    1: 'GPSLatitudeRef',   # N or S
    2: 'GPSLatitude',      # Degrees, minutes, seconds
    3: 'GPSLongitudeRef',  # E or W
    4: 'GPSLongitude',     # Degrees, minutes, seconds
    6: 'GPSAltitude',      # Metres above sea level
}

for tag_id, name in COMMON_TAGS.items():
    print(f"  Tag {tag_id:<6} = {name}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: Tag 306 = DateTime, Tag 271 = Make, Tag 272 = Model, Tag 34853 = GPSInfo. These are the BIG FOUR for forensic investigations!

key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF stores GPS coordinates in Degrees, Minutes, Seconds (DMS) format: 51 degrees 30 minutes 26.4 seconds North, 0 degrees 7 minutes 40.0 seconds West.

To plot on a map or use in analysis, you need DECIMAL DEGREES: 51.507333, -0.127778.

Conversion formula: decimal = degrees + (minutes / 60) + (seconds / 3600). If the reference is S (south) or W (west), the result is NEGATIVE.

This conversion has caught criminals who posted photos online without realising the GPS coordinates pinpointed their exact location.
key_concepts.example:
def dms_to_decimal(degrees, minutes, seconds, ref):
    """Convert GPS DMS to decimal degrees.
    
    Args:
        degrees: Whole degrees (e.g., 51)
        minutes: Arc minutes (e.g., 30)
        seconds: Arc seconds (e.g., 26.4)
        ref: Reference direction ('N','S','E','W')
    
    Returns:
        float: Decimal degrees (negative for S/W)
    """
    decimal = degrees + (minutes / 60) + (seconds / 3600)
    if ref in ('S', 'W'):
        decimal = -decimal
    return round(decimal, 6)

# Example: London coordinates
lat = dms_to_decimal(51, 30, 26.46, 'N')
lon = dms_to_decimal(0, 7, 40.0, 'W')
print(f"London: {lat}, {lon}")
print(f"Google Maps: https://maps.google.com/?q={lat},{lon}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: DMS to decimal: degrees + minutes/60 + seconds/3600. South and West are NEGATIVE. This formula is essential for forensic mapping!

key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF contains THREE timestamp fields: DateTime (when last modified), DateTimeOriginal (when the photo was first captured), and DateTimeDigitized (when digitised from film/scanner).

For forensics, DateTimeOriginal is most important — it records when the shutter actually fired. DateTime can be changed by editing software, but DateTimeOriginal is harder to fake.

Comparing timestamps across multiple photos builds a TIMELINE: where was the suspect at 14:00, 14:15, 14:30? Combined with GPS, you get a complete movement trail.

WARNING: Camera clocks can be wrong! Verify by cross-referencing with other evidence (CCTV, phone records, access logs).
key_concepts.example:
from datetime import datetime

# EXIF timestamp format: 'YYYY:MM:DD HH:MM:SS'
def parse_exif_datetime(dt_string):
    """Parse EXIF datetime to Python datetime object."""
    return datetime.strptime(dt_string, '%Y:%m:%d %H:%M:%S')

# Timeline from multiple photos
photos = [
    {'file': 'IMG_001.jpg', 'datetime': '2025:02:28 08:15:30', 'gps': '51.5074, -0.1278'},
    {'file': 'IMG_002.jpg', 'datetime': '2025:02:28 09:30:00', 'gps': '51.5155, -0.1420'},
    {'file': 'IMG_003.jpg', 'datetime': '2025:02:28 11:45:15', 'gps': '51.5033, -0.1195'},
]

print('PHOTO TIMELINE')
for p in photos:
    dt = parse_exif_datetime(p['datetime'])
    print(f"  {dt.strftime('%H:%M')} | {p['file']} | {p['gps']}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: EXIF uses 'YYYY:MM:DD HH:MM:SS' format (colons in date!). DateTimeOriginal = when REALLY taken. DateTime = when last saved. The difference reveals editing!

key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF records the camera Make (manufacturer) and Model. Some cameras also embed a unique serial number (BodySerialNumber tag). This creates a DEVICE FINGERPRINT.

If you find photos at a crime scene and on a suspect's phone, matching Make + Model + SerialNumber proves they came from the SAME device. Even without a serial number, the combination of Make + Model + lens characteristics + sensor noise pattern can identify devices.

Additionally, the Software tag reveals which app or program last saved the image — useful for detecting tampering (Photoshop vs original camera software).
key_concepts.example:
# Camera identification from EXIF
def identify_camera(exif_dict):
    """Extract device fingerprint from EXIF."""
    make = exif_dict.get('Make', 'Unknown')
    model = exif_dict.get('Model', 'Unknown')
    serial = exif_dict.get('BodySerialNumber', 'N/A')
    software = exif_dict.get('Software', 'Unknown')
    
    return {
        'device': f"{make} {model}",
        'serial': serial,
        'software': software,
        'fingerprint': f"{make}_{model}_{serial}".replace(' ', '_')
    }

# Example
exif = {'Make': 'Apple', 'Model': 'iPhone 15 Pro', 
        'BodySerialNumber': 'DNQXYZ123', 'Software': 'Photos 9.0'}
cam = identify_camera(exif)
print(f"Device:      {cam['device']}")
print(f"Serial:      {cam['serial']}")
print(f"Software:    {cam['software']}")
print(f"Fingerprint: {cam['fingerprint']}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: Make + Model + SerialNumber = device fingerprint. This links photos to a SPECIFIC camera. Software tag shows if the image was edited!

key_concepts.explanation:
When EXIF is ABSENT from a photo, that itself is evidence. Modern smartphones ALWAYS embed EXIF. If a JPEG from a phone has zero EXIF data, someone deliberately removed it.

Different stripping methods leave different traces: social media platforms strip EXIF but leave platform-specific markers. Photoshop changes the Software tag. Some tools remove all metadata but leave the JFIF header. Complete stripping (no APP1 segment at all) suggests deliberate anti-forensic action.

Even after stripping, some data survives: the image dimensions, colour profile, and compression quality can still reveal the source device or software.
key_concepts.example:
# Detect metadata stripping
def analyse_metadata_status(filepath):
    """Check if EXIF has been stripped."""
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()
    
    results = []
    # Check for EXIF APP1 marker
    has_exif = b'\xff\xe1' in data[:1000]
    results.append(f"EXIF present: {has_exif}")
    
    # Check for JFIF marker
    has_jfif = b'JFIF' in data[:20]
    results.append(f"JFIF header: {has_jfif}")
    
    # Check for known software signatures
    if b'Photoshop' in data:
        results.append("Software: Adobe Photoshop detected")
    if b'GIMP' in data:
        results.append("Software: GIMP detected")
    if not has_exif and data[:3] == b'\xff\xd8\xff':
        results.append("WARNING: JPEG without EXIF — metadata likely stripped!")
    
    return results
key_concepts.ai_hint: No EXIF on a smartphone photo = someone stripped it. ABSENCE of metadata IS evidence. Different tools leave different traces!

key_concepts.explanation:
Pillow (PIL Fork) is the main Python library for image processing. Install with: pip install Pillow. It reads image properties, EXIF data, and pixel data.

Key methods: Image.open('photo.jpg') opens the image. img._getexif() returns raw EXIF as a dictionary of tag numbers to values. ExifTags.TAGS converts tag numbers to names. ExifTags.GPSTAGS converts GPS tag numbers to names.

For forensic work, you combine Pillow with hashlib (integrity), os (file operations), and datetime (timeline analysis) to build complete evidence processing tools.
key_concepts.example:
# Pillow EXIF extraction (run on VM with real images)
from PIL import Image
from PIL.ExifTags import TAGS, GPSTAGS

def extract_exif(filepath):
    """Extract all EXIF data from an image."""
    img = Image.open(filepath)
    exif_raw = img._getexif()
    if exif_raw is None:
        return {}  # No EXIF data
    
    exif_named = {}
    for tag_id, value in exif_raw.items():
        tag_name = TAGS.get(tag_id, f'Unknown_{tag_id}')
        exif_named[tag_name] = value
    return exif_named

# Usage on VM:
# exif = extract_exif('photo.jpg')
# for key, value in exif.items():
#     print(f'{key}: {value}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: pip install Pillow on your VM. img._getexif() gets raw tags. ExifTags.TAGS maps numbers to names. This is your forensic image toolkit!

key_concepts.explanation:
Real forensic cases involve hundreds or thousands of photos from seized devices. You need automated tools that process entire directories: extract EXIF from every image, build timelines, identify unique devices, flag anomalies, and generate reports.

The pattern: os.walk() to find images → extract EXIF from each → collect into a data structure → analyse (timelines, device linking, GPS mapping) → generate CSV/JSON report.

This is where your file I/O, function, and loop skills combine into real forensic capability.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Batch processing turns you from manual analyst to automated forensic tool operator. Process 10,000 photos in seconds instead of days!

key_concepts.concept: Privacy Implications — What Your Photos Reveal
key_concepts.explanation:
Understanding EXIF has a dual purpose: forensic investigation AND personal privacy protection.

Your smartphone photos reveal: your home location (photos taken at home), your workplace, your daily routine (timestamps), your device model (Make/Model), places you visit, people you photograph (if facial recognition is applied), and more.

Protection: disable GPS in camera settings, use metadata stripping tools before sharing, be aware that social media strips EXIF (but keeps a copy). On iOS: Settings > Privacy > Location Services > Camera. On Android: Camera > Settings > Location tags.

For forensics, this privacy awareness helps you understand what evidence is available and what suspects might try to hide.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Your photos create a detailed profile: where you live, work, travel, and what devices you own. Forensic power for investigators, privacy risk for everyone else!

key_concepts.explanation:
EXIF metadata is admissible as evidence in court, but with caveats: the chain of custody must be documented, the extraction method must be reproducible, and the analyst must acknowledge that EXIF CAN be manipulated (timestamps can be changed, GPS can be spoofed).

Best forensic approach: hash the original file BEFORE extraction (proves it was not modified), use validated tools (ExifTool, Pillow with documented versions), record every step, and cross-reference EXIF data with other evidence sources (CCTV, phone records, witness statements).

Never rely on EXIF alone — it corroborates other evidence, but should not be the sole proof.
key_concepts.example:
# Forensic EXIF extraction with integrity verification
import hashlib

def forensic_extract(filepath):
    """Court-ready EXIF extraction with hash verification."""
    # Step 1: Hash BEFORE touching
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        original_hash = hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()
    
    # Step 2: Extract EXIF (read-only operation)
    # exif = extract_exif(filepath)
    exif = {'Make': 'Apple', 'Model': 'iPhone 15'}  # Simulated
    
    # Step 3: Hash AFTER extraction (must match!)
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        post_hash = hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()
    
    # Step 4: Verify integrity
    integrity = original_hash == post_hash
    
    return {
        'file': filepath,
        'hash_before': original_hash,
        'hash_after': post_hash,
        'integrity_verified': integrity,
        'exif': exif
    }
key_concepts.ai_hint: Hash BEFORE and AFTER extraction. If hashes match, you proved the file was not modified during analysis. This is MANDATORY for court!

key_concepts.concept: Working with struct — Low-Level Binary Parsing
key_concepts.explanation:
For deep forensic analysis, sometimes you need to parse EXIF at the byte level using Python's struct module. This lets you read specific bytes from the file and interpret them as numbers.

struct.unpack('>H', data) reads 2 bytes as a big-endian unsigned short. struct.unpack('<I', data) reads 4 bytes as a little-endian unsigned int.

JPEG segments use big-endian (Motorola byte order). But the TIFF block inside EXIF can be either big-endian (starts with 'MM') or little-endian (starts with 'II').

This low-level approach is essential when standard libraries fail — corrupted files, non-standard EXIF, or proprietary metadata formats.
key_concepts.example:
import struct

# Parse JPEG segment markers
def read_jpeg_segments(filepath):
    """Read JPEG segment markers from file."""
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        # Read SOI (Start of Image)
        soi = f.read(2)
        if soi != b'\xff\xd8':
            return 'Not a JPEG'
        
        segments = []
        while True:
            marker = f.read(2)
            if len(marker) < 2:
                break
            if marker[0] != 0xFF:
                break
            
            # Read segment length (big-endian 2 bytes)
            length_bytes = f.read(2)
            if len(length_bytes) < 2:
                break
            length = struct.unpack('>H', length_bytes)[0]
            
            segment_name = f'0x{marker[1]:02X}'
            if marker[1] == 0xE1:
                segment_name = 'APP1 (EXIF)'
            elif marker[1] == 0xE0:
                segment_name = 'APP0 (JFIF)'
            
            segments.append((segment_name, length))
            f.seek(length - 2, 1)  # Skip segment data
    
    return segments
key_concepts.ai_hint: struct.unpack reads raw bytes as numbers. '>H' = big-endian 2-byte unsigned. This is how you parse file formats at the lowest level!

[IMAGE]

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This example works entirely in memory — no real images needed! It shows the STRUCTURE of EXIF data:

1. DEVICE section: identifies the camera (Apple iPhone 15 Pro Max).
2. TIMESTAMPS: three time fields that should match for unedited photos.
3. CAMERA SETTINGS: exposure, aperture, ISO — tells you lighting conditions.
4. IMAGE: dimensions and megapixels.

When all three timestamps match, the image has not been re-saved. If DateTime differs from DateTimeOriginal, the image was edited after capture.

Notice the Software field shows '17.3.1' (iOS version) — this tells you the OS version on the phone!

complete_examples.description: Convert EXIF GPS from DMS format to decimal degrees with map links

# GPS Coordinate Converter
# EXIF stores GPS as (degrees, minutes, seconds) tuples
# We need decimal degrees for mapping

def exif_gps_to_decimal(gps_data, ref):
    """Convert EXIF GPS tuple to decimal degrees.
    
    EXIF format: ((degrees, denom), (minutes, denom), (seconds, denom))
    Some cameras use: (degrees, minutes, seconds) as simple numbers
    """
    if isinstance(gps_data[0], tuple):
        # Rational format: ((51, 1), (30, 1), (2646, 100))
        degrees = gps_data[0][0] / gps_data[0][1]
        minutes = gps_data[1][0] / gps_data[1][1]
        seconds = gps_data[2][0] / gps_data[2][1]
    else:
        # Simple format: (51, 30, 26.46)
        degrees, minutes, seconds = gps_data
    
    decimal = degrees + (minutes / 60) + (seconds / 3600)
    if ref in ('S', 'W'):
        decimal = -decimal
    return round(decimal, 6)

# Test with known locations
locations = [
    {
        'name': 'London (Big Ben)',
        'lat_data': ((51, 1), (30, 1), (264, 10)),
        'lat_ref': 'N',
        'lon_data': ((0, 1), (7, 1), (3950, 100)),
        'lon_ref': 'W'
    },
    {
        'name': 'New York (Statue of Liberty)',
        'lat_data': (40, 41, 21.24),
        'lat_ref': 'N',
        'lon_data': (74, 2, 40.08),
        'lon_ref': 'W'
    },
    {
        'name': 'Tokyo (Tokyo Tower)',
        'lat_data': (35, 39, 31.0),
        'lat_ref': 'N',
        'lon_data': (139, 44, 43.0),
        'lon_ref': 'E'
    },
    {
        'name': 'Sydney (Opera House)',
        'lat_data': (33, 51, 25.0),
        'lat_ref': 'S',
        'lon_data': (151, 12, 55.0),
        'lon_ref': 'E'
    }
]

print("GPS COORDINATE CONVERSION")
print("=" * 65)
for loc in locations:
    lat = exif_gps_to_decimal(loc['lat_data'], loc['lat_ref'])
    lon = exif_gps_to_decimal(loc['lon_data'], loc['lon_ref'])
    print(f"\n{loc['name']}:")
    print(f"  Decimal: {lat}, {lon}")
    print(f"  Map:     https://maps.google.com/?q={lat},{lon}")

  Map:     https://maps.google.com/?q=51.5074,-0.1276

New York (Statue of Liberty):

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
GPS conversion handles TWO formats found in EXIF:

1. RATIONAL format: ((51, 1), (30, 1), (264, 10)) — each value is (numerator, denominator). 264/10 = 26.4 seconds.
2. SIMPLE format: (40, 41, 21.24) — plain numbers for degrees, minutes, seconds.

The function handles both! South and West become negative values.

The Google Maps link is generated automatically — click it to see exactly where the photo was taken. In a real investigation, this reveals the suspect's location at the exact time recorded in DateTimeOriginal.

This has literally solved crimes. Ransom photos, stolen goods listings on eBay, even metadata in uploaded documents.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Image File Header Analyser
complete_examples.description: Read binary headers to identify image types and detect disguised files
complete_examples.code:
import os
import struct

IMAGE_SIGS = {
    b'\xff\xd8\xff': ('JPEG', 'Joint Photographic Experts Group'),
    b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n': ('PNG', 'Portable Network Graphics'),
    b'GIF89a': ('GIF89a', 'Graphics Interchange Format (animated)'),
    b'GIF87a': ('GIF87a', 'Graphics Interchange Format'),
    b'BM': ('BMP', 'Bitmap'),
    b'II*\x00': ('TIFF-LE', 'Tagged Image File Format (little-endian)'),
    b'MM\x00*': ('TIFF-BE', 'Tagged Image File Format (big-endian)'),
    b'RIFF': ('WEBP?', 'Possible WebP (check further)'),
}

def analyse_image_header(filepath):
    """Deep analysis of image file header."""
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        header = f.read(32)
        f.seek(0, 2)
        file_size = f.tell()
    
    result = {
        'file': os.path.basename(filepath),
        'size': file_size,
        'extension': os.path.splitext(filepath)[1],
        'type': 'Unknown',
        'description': 'Unrecognised format',
        'has_exif': False,
        'alerts': []
    }
    
    # Identify by magic bytes
    for sig, (name, desc) in IMAGE_SIGS.items():
        if header[:len(sig)] == sig:
            result['type'] = name
            result['description'] = desc
            break
    
    # JPEG-specific analysis
    if header[:3] == b'\xff\xd8\xff':
        # Check for EXIF (APP1 marker)
        result['has_exif'] = b'\xff\xe1' in header or b'Exif' in header
        # Check for JFIF
        has_jfif = b'JFIF' in header[:20]
        result['jfif'] = has_jfif
    
    # Extension mismatch detection
    ext_map = {'.jpg': 'JPEG', '.jpeg': 'JPEG', '.png': 'PNG',
               '.gif': 'GIF89a', '.bmp': 'BMP', '.tiff': 'TIFF-LE'}
    expected = ext_map.get(result['extension'].lower())
    if expected and expected != result['type'] and result['type'] != 'Unknown':
        result['alerts'].append(
            f"MISMATCH: Extension {result['extension']} but content is {result['type']}!"
        )
    
    return result

# Create test files
test_files = [
    ('photo.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + b'\x00' * 20),
    ('image.png', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + b'\x00' * 24),
    ('fake_image.jpg', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + b'\x00' * 24),  # PNG disguised as JPG!
    ('document.jpg', b'%PDF-1.7' + b'\x00' * 24),  # PDF disguised as JPG!
    ('screenshot.png', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + b'\x00' * 24),
]

for name, content in test_files:
    with open(name, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

print("IMAGE HEADER ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 60)
for name, _ in test_files:
    r = analyse_image_header(name)
    exif_icon = 'Y' if r['has_exif'] else 'N'
    print(f"\n{r['file']}:")
    print(f"  Type: {r['type']} ({r['description']})")
    print(f"  Size: {r['size']} bytes  |  EXIF: {exif_icon}")
    for alert in r['alerts']:
        print(f"  >>> ALERT: {alert}")

# Cleanup
for name, _ in test_files:
    os.remove(name)
complete_examples.output:
IMAGE HEADER ANALYSIS
============================================================

photo.jpg:
  Type: JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group)
  Size: 32 bytes  |  EXIF: Y

image.png:
  Type: PNG (Portable Network Graphics)
  Size: 32 bytes  |  EXIF: N

fake_image.jpg:
  Type: PNG (Portable Network Graphics)
  Size: 32 bytes  |  EXIF: N
  >>> ALERT: MISMATCH: Extension .jpg but content is PNG!

document.jpg:
  Type: Unknown (Unrecognised format)
  Size: 32 bytes  |  EXIF: N
  >>> ALERT: MISMATCH: Extension .jpg but content is Unknown!
...
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This catches disguised files — critical in forensics:

1. fake_image.jpg has a .jpg extension but PNG magic bytes — someone renamed it!
2. document.jpg is actually a PDF (starts with %PDF) — hidden in plain sight!
3. photo.jpg is a genuine JPEG WITH EXIF data (has the APP1/Exif markers).
4. screenshot.png is a real PNG — screenshots typically have NO EXIF data.

The struct module can parse further: JPEG segment lengths, TIFF headers inside EXIF, and more. But even this simple magic byte check catches most file disguises.

On Kali VM: file *.jpg uses the same magic byte analysis. Try renaming a PNG to .jpg and run file on it!

complete_examples.title: Example 4: EXIF Timestamp Timeline Builder

complete_examples.code:
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def parse_exif_dt(dt_str):
    """Parse EXIF datetime string."""
    return datetime.strptime(dt_str, '%Y:%m:%d %H:%M:%S')

def time_gap(dt1, dt2):
    """Calculate time gap between two datetimes."""
    diff = abs(dt2 - dt1)
    hours = diff.seconds // 3600
    minutes = (diff.seconds % 3600) // 60
    if diff.days > 0:
        return f"{diff.days}d {hours}h"
    elif hours > 0:
        return f"{hours}h {minutes}m"
    else:
        return f"{minutes}m"

# Simulated photo collection from a seized phone
photos = [
    {'file': 'IMG_4201.jpg', 'dt_original': '2025:02:28 07:45:12',
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 07:45:12', 'gps': '51.5074, -0.1278', 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
    {'file': 'IMG_4202.jpg', 'dt_original': '2025:02:28 08:30:00',
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 08:30:00', 'gps': '51.5155, -0.1420', 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
    {'file': 'IMG_4203.jpg', 'dt_original': '2025:02:28 09:15:45',
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 12:00:00', 'gps': '51.5033, -0.1195', 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
    {'file': 'IMG_4204.jpg', 'dt_original': '2025:02:28 11:00:00',
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 11:00:00', 'gps': '51.4975, -0.1357', 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
    {'file': 'IMG_4205.jpg', 'dt_original': '2025:02:28 14:20:33',
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 14:20:33', 'gps': '51.5074, -0.1278', 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
    {'file': 'Screenshot_01.png', 'dt_original': None,
     'dt_modified': '2025:02:28 10:30:00', 'gps': None, 'device': 'iPhone 15'},
]

# Sort by original timestamp (put None at end)
photos_sorted = sorted(photos, key=lambda p: p['dt_original'] or '9999')

print("PHOTO TIMELINE ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"{'Time':<8} {'File':<20} {'Location':<22} {'Flags'}")
print("-" * 70)

prev_dt = None
for p in photos_sorted:
    flags = []
    
    if p['dt_original'] is None:
        print(f"{'N/A':<8} {p['file']:<20} {'No GPS':<22} NO EXIF (screenshot?)")
        continue
    
    dt_orig = parse_exif_dt(p['dt_original'])
    dt_mod = parse_exif_dt(p['dt_modified'])
    
    # Flag: time gap analysis
    gap_str = ''
    if prev_dt:
        gap = dt_orig - prev_dt
        gap_str = f"(+{time_gap(prev_dt, dt_orig)})"
    
    # Flag: edited (timestamps differ)
    if p['dt_original'] != p['dt_modified']:
        flags.append('EDITED!')
    
    # Flag: returned to same location
    if p['gps'] == photos_sorted[0].get('gps') and p != photos_sorted[0]:
        flags.append('RETURNED')
    
    loc = p['gps'] if p['gps'] else 'No GPS'
    flag_str = ', '.join(flags) if flags else ''
    
    print(f"{dt_orig.strftime('%H:%M'):<8} {p['file']:<20} {loc:<22} {gap_str} {flag_str}")
    prev_dt = dt_orig

print("-" * 70)
print(f"\nANALYSIS:")
edited = [p for p in photos if p['dt_original'] and p['dt_original'] != p['dt_modified']]
if edited:
    print(f"  EDITED FILES ({len(edited)}):")
    for p in edited:
        print(f"    {p['file']}: Original {p['dt_original']} vs Modified {p['dt_modified']}")

no_exif = [p for p in photos if p['dt_original'] is None]
if no_exif:
    print(f"  NO EXIF ({len(no_exif)}): {', '.join(p['file'] for p in no_exif)}")
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Timeline analysis reveals movement patterns and anomalies:

1. TIME GAPS show where the subject was NOT taking photos (3h 20m gap = doing what?).
2. EDITED flag: IMG_4203 was taken at 09:15 but modified at 12:00 — someone edited it later. WHY?
3. RETURNED flag: subject started at 51.5074 and came back — their home/office?
4. NO EXIF: Screenshot has no original timestamp — different evidence type.

This exact analysis is used in criminal investigations: proving someone was at a location, identifying suspicious gaps in activity, and catching photo manipulation.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Camera Device Linker
complete_examples.description: Link photos to specific devices by analysing camera metadata
complete_examples.code:
# Camera Device Linker — identify unique devices across photo collections

def create_device_fingerprint(exif):
    """Create a unique device identifier from EXIF data."""
    make = exif.get('Make', '').strip()
    model = exif.get('Model', '').strip()
    serial = exif.get('BodySerialNumber', '')
    software = exif.get('Software', '')
    lens = exif.get('LensModel', '')
    
    # Primary fingerprint (strong)
    if serial:
        return f"{make}|{model}|SN:{serial}", 'STRONG'
    # Secondary fingerprint (moderate)
    elif lens:
        return f"{make}|{model}|{lens}", 'MODERATE'
    # Weak fingerprint
    else:
        return f"{make}|{model}|{software}", 'WEAK'

# Photo collection from a case
photos = [
    {'file': 'crime_scene_01.jpg', 'Make': 'Canon', 'Model': 'EOS R5',
     'BodySerialNumber': '012345678', 'Software': 'Firmware 1.8.1', 'LensModel': 'RF 24-70mm'},
    {'file': 'crime_scene_02.jpg', 'Make': 'Canon', 'Model': 'EOS R5',
     'BodySerialNumber': '012345678', 'Software': 'Firmware 1.8.1', 'LensModel': 'RF 24-70mm'},
    {'file': 'suspect_phone_01.jpg', 'Make': 'Samsung', 'Model': 'SM-S928B',
     'BodySerialNumber': '', 'Software': 'S928BXXU1AXA1', 'LensModel': ''},
    {'file': 'suspect_phone_02.jpg', 'Make': 'Samsung', 'Model': 'SM-S928B',
     'BodySerialNumber': '', 'Software': 'S928BXXU1AXA1', 'LensModel': ''},
    {'file': 'anonymous_01.jpg', 'Make': '', 'Model': '', 'BodySerialNumber': '',
     'Software': 'Adobe Photoshop 25.0', 'LensModel': ''},
    {'file': 'evidence_03.jpg', 'Make': 'Canon', 'Model': 'EOS R5',
     'BodySerialNumber': '012345678', 'Software': 'Adobe Lightroom 7.1', 'LensModel': 'RF 24-70mm'},
]

# Group by device
devices = {}
for photo in photos:
    fp, strength = create_device_fingerprint(photo)
    if fp not in devices:
        devices[fp] = {'strength': strength, 'photos': [], 'fingerprint': fp}
    devices[fp]['photos'].append(photo)

# Report
print("DEVICE LINKAGE ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 60)
for i, (fp, info) in enumerate(devices.items(), 1):
    print(f"\nDevice {i} [{info['strength']}]:")
    print(f"  Fingerprint: {fp}")
    print(f"  Photos ({len(info['photos'])}):")
    for p in info['photos']:
        sw = p.get('Software', '')
        edited = ' [EDITED]' if 'Photoshop' in sw or 'Lightroom' in sw else ''
        print(f"    {p['file']}{edited}")

print(f"\nSUMMARY: {len(devices)} unique devices identified")

# Cross-reference alert
canon_photos = [p for p in photos if p.get('BodySerialNumber') == '012345678']
if len(canon_photos) > 1:
    edited_canon = [p for p in canon_photos if 'Photoshop' in p.get('Software', '') or 'Lightroom' in p.get('Software', '')]
    if edited_canon:
        print(f"\nALERT: Canon SN:012345678 has {len(edited_canon)} edited photo(s):")
        for p in edited_canon:
            print(f"  {p['file']} — edited with {p['Software']}")

Device linking is powerful forensic evidence:

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Metadata Stripping Detector
complete_examples.description: Detect when EXIF has been deliberately removed from images
complete_examples.code:
import os

def analyse_metadata_presence(filepath):
    """Detect if metadata has been stripped from a JPEG."""
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()
    
    filename = os.path.basename(filepath)
    ext = os.path.splitext(filename)[1].lower()
    size = len(data)
    
    findings = {
        'file': filename,
        'size': size,
        'is_jpeg': data[:3] == b'\xff\xd8\xff',
        'has_exif': False,
        'has_jfif': False,
        'has_xmp': False,
        'software_detected': None,
        'suspicion_level': 'NONE',
        'notes': []
    }
    
    if not findings['is_jpeg']:
        if ext in ('.jpg', '.jpeg'):
            findings['notes'].append('NOT A JPEG despite extension!')
            findings['suspicion_level'] = 'HIGH'
        return findings
    
    # Check for EXIF (APP1 with Exif header)
    findings['has_exif'] = b'Exif\x00\x00' in data[:500]
    
    # Check for JFIF
    findings['has_jfif'] = b'JFIF' in data[:20]
    
    # Check for XMP metadata
    findings['has_xmp'] = b'http://ns.adobe.com/xap/' in data
    
    # Detect known software signatures
    sw_sigs = [
        (b'Adobe Photoshop', 'Adobe Photoshop'),
        (b'GIMP', 'GIMP'),
        (b'Paint.NET', 'Paint.NET'),
        (b'Snapseed', 'Snapseed'),
        (b'Instagram', 'Instagram'),
    ]
    for sig, name in sw_sigs:
        if sig in data:
            findings['software_detected'] = name
            break
    
    # Suspicion analysis
    if findings['is_jpeg'] and not findings['has_exif']:
        if size > 100000:  # Large JPEG with no EXIF
            findings['suspicion_level'] = 'HIGH'
            findings['notes'].append('Large JPEG with NO EXIF — metadata likely stripped!')
        else:
            findings['suspicion_level'] = 'MEDIUM'
            findings['notes'].append('JPEG without EXIF — could be screenshot or stripped.')
    
    if findings['software_detected']:
        findings['notes'].append(f"Processed by: {findings['software_detected']}")
    
    if findings['has_exif']:
        findings['suspicion_level'] = 'LOW'
        findings['notes'].append('EXIF present — metadata intact.')
    
    return findings

# Create test images
test_images = [
    ('normal_photo.jpg',
     b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + b'\x00' * 200000),
    ('stripped.jpg',
     b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF\x00\x01' + b'\x00' * 200000),
    ('photoshopped.jpg',
     b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF\x00\x01' + b'Adobe Photoshop' + b'\x00' * 200000),
    ('tiny_crop.jpg',
     b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF\x00\x01' + b'\x00' * 500),
    ('fake_jpg.jpg',
     b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + b'\x00' * 1000),
]

for name, content in test_images:
    with open(name, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

print("METADATA STRIPPING ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 60)
for name, _ in test_images:
    r = analyse_metadata_presence(name)
    icon = {'NONE': '-', 'LOW': 'OK', 'MEDIUM': '?', 'HIGH': '!!'}[r['suspicion_level']]
    print(f"\n[{icon}] {r['file']} ({r['size']:,} bytes)")
    print(f"    EXIF: {r['has_exif']}  JFIF: {r['has_jfif']}  XMP: {r['has_xmp']}")
    for note in r['notes']:
        print(f"    > {note}")

# Cleanup
for name, _ in test_images:
    os.remove(name)

============================================================

[OK] normal_photo.jpg (200,012 bytes)
    EXIF: True  JFIF: False  XMP: False
    > EXIF present — metadata intact.

[!!] stripped.jpg (200,012 bytes)
    EXIF: False  JFIF: True  XMP: False
    > Large JPEG with NO EXIF — metadata likely stripped!

[!!] photoshopped.jpg (200,027 bytes)
    EXIF: False  JFIF: True  XMP: False
    > Large JPEG with NO EXIF — metadata likely stripped!
    > Processed by: Adobe Photoshop

[?] tiny_crop.jpg (512 bytes)
    EXIF: False  JFIF: True  XMP: False
    > JPEG without EXIF — could be screenshot or stripped.

[!!] fake_jpg.jpg (1,008 bytes)

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Metadata stripping detection catches anti-forensic behaviour:

1. normal_photo.jpg: Has EXIF — genuine camera photo, metadata intact.
2. stripped.jpg: 200KB JPEG with NO EXIF — phones ALWAYS add EXIF. Someone deliberately removed it!
3. photoshopped.jpg: No EXIF + Photoshop signature — edited AND stripped. Double suspicious.
4. tiny_crop.jpg: Small and no EXIF — could be a crop from a messaging app.
5. fake_jpg.jpg: Not even a JPEG — PNG bytes with .jpg extension!

The ABSENCE of metadata is itself evidence. A 200KB smartphone JPEG should have 5-10KB of EXIF. If it does not, someone removed it deliberately.

        'format': img.format,
        'mode': img.mode,
        'size': f"{img.size[0]}x{img.size[1]}",
        'megapixels': round(img.size[0] * img.size[1] / 1e6, 1),
    }
    
    # EXIF data
    exif_raw = img._getexif()
    if exif_raw is None:
        info['exif'] = 'NO EXIF DATA'
        return info
    
    exif_named = {}
    gps_data = {}
    
    for tag_id, value in exif_raw.items():
        tag_name = TAGS.get(tag_id, f'Tag_{tag_id}')
        
        if tag_name == 'GPSInfo':
            for gps_id, gps_val in value.items():
                gps_name = GPSTAGS.get(gps_id, f'GPSTag_{gps_id}')
                gps_data[gps_name] = gps_val
        elif isinstance(value, bytes):
            exif_named[tag_name] = f'<bytes ({len(value)} bytes)>'
        else:
            exif_named[tag_name] = value
    
    info['exif'] = exif_named
    info['gps'] = gps_data if gps_data else 'No GPS'
    return info

def format_gps(gps_data):
    """Convert GPS dict to decimal coordinates."""
    if not isinstance(gps_data, dict) or 'GPSLatitude' not in gps_data:
        return None
    
    def to_decimal(dms, ref):
        d = float(dms[0])
        m = float(dms[1])
        s = float(dms[2])
        decimal = d + m/60 + s/3600
        if ref in ('S', 'W'):
            decimal = -decimal
        return round(decimal, 6)
    
    lat = to_decimal(gps_data['GPSLatitude'], gps_data.get('GPSLatitudeRef', 'N'))
    lon = to_decimal(gps_data['GPSLongitude'], gps_data.get('GPSLongitudeRef', 'E'))
    return lat, lon

import csv
import os
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime

def process_photo(filepath):
    """Extract metadata from a simulated photo."""
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()
    
    sha = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
    is_jpeg = data[:3] == b'\xff\xd8\xff'
    has_exif = b'Exif' in data[:500] if is_jpeg else False
    
    return {
        'filename': os.path.basename(filepath),
        'size': len(data),
        'sha256': sha,
        'is_jpeg': is_jpeg,
        'has_exif': has_exif,
    }

def generate_csv_report(photos, output_file):
    """Generate CSV evidence report."""
    fields = ['filename', 'size', 'sha256', 'is_jpeg', 'has_exif']
    with open(output_file, 'w', newline='') as f:
        writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fields)
        writer.writeheader()
        for photo in photos:
            writer.writerow(photo)
    return output_file

# Create test photo files
os.makedirs('evidence_photos', exist_ok=True)
test_photos = [
    ('evidence_photos/IMG_001.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + os.urandom(5000)),
    ('evidence_photos/IMG_002.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + os.urandom(8000)),
    ('evidence_photos/IMG_003.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF' + os.urandom(3000)),
    ('evidence_photos/screenshot.png', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + os.urandom(2000)),
    ('evidence_photos/edited.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF' + os.urandom(12000)),
]

for path, content in test_photos:
    with open(path, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

# Process all photos
results = []
for path, _ in test_photos:
    results.append(process_photo(path))

# Generate report
report = generate_csv_report(results, 'photo_report.csv')

# Display summary
print("BATCH PHOTO PROCESSING REPORT")
print("=" * 55)
print(f"Photos processed: {len(results)}")
jpegs = sum(1 for r in results if r['is_jpeg'])
with_exif = sum(1 for r in results if r['has_exif'])
print(f"JPEG files:       {jpegs}")
print(f"With EXIF:        {with_exif}")
print(f"Without EXIF:     {jpegs - with_exif} (potentially stripped!)")
print(f"Non-JPEG:         {len(results) - jpegs}")
total_size = sum(r['size'] for r in results)
print(f"Total size:       {total_size:,} bytes")
print(f"Report saved:     {report}")

print(f"\nFILE DETAILS:")
for r in results:
    exif_status = 'EXIF' if r['has_exif'] else 'NO EXIF' if r['is_jpeg'] else 'N/A'
    print(f"  {r['filename']:<25} {r['size']:>8,}B  {exif_status:<8} {r['sha256'][:12]}...")

# Cleanup
import shutil
shutil.rmtree('evidence_photos')
os.remove('photo_report.csv')

# Privacy Audit — what do your photos reveal about you?

from collections import Counter
from datetime import datetime

def privacy_audit(photos):
    """Analyse what a photo collection reveals about the owner."""
    devices = Counter()
    locations = []
    times = []
    software = Counter()
    
    for p in photos:
        if p.get('Make') and p.get('Model'):
            devices[f"{p['Make']} {p['Model']}"] += 1
        if p.get('GPS'):
            locations.append(p['GPS'])
        if p.get('DateTime'):
            try:
                dt = datetime.strptime(p['DateTime'], '%Y:%m:%d %H:%M:%S')
                times.append(dt)
            except ValueError:
                pass
        if p.get('Software'):
            software[p['Software']] += 1
    
    # Analyse patterns
    print("PRIVACY AUDIT REPORT")
    print("=" * 55)
    
    # Device inventory
    print(f"\n[DEVICES] ({len(devices)} unique)")
    for dev, count in devices.most_common():
        print(f"  {dev}: {count} photos")
    
    # Location analysis
    print(f"\n[LOCATIONS] ({len(locations)} geotagged photos)")
    if locations:
        unique_locs = len(set(str(l) for l in locations))
        print(f"  Unique locations: {unique_locs}")
        # Most visited
        loc_counts = Counter(str(l) for l in locations)
        top = loc_counts.most_common(1)[0]
        print(f"  Most visited: {top[0]} ({top[1]} photos)")
        print(f"  >>> This could be HOME or WORK!")
    else:
        print(f"  No GPS data (good for privacy!)")
    
    # Time patterns
    print(f"\n[TIME PATTERNS] ({len(times)} timestamped)")
    if times:
        hours = Counter(dt.hour for dt in times)
        print(f"  Date range: {min(times).date()} to {max(times).date()}")
        most_active = hours.most_common(1)[0]
        print(f"  Most active hour: {most_active[0]:02d}:00 ({most_active[1]} photos)")
        weekend = sum(1 for dt in times if dt.weekday() >= 5)
        weekday = len(times) - weekend
        print(f"  Weekday: {weekday} | Weekend: {weekend}")
    
    # Risk assessment
    risk = 0
    print(f"\n[RISK ASSESSMENT]")
    if len(locations) > 5:
        risk += 3
        print(f"  HIGH: {len(locations)} geotagged photos reveal movement pattern")
    if len(devices) > 0:
        risk += 1
        print(f"  LOW: Device info reveals what technology you own")
    if len(times) > 10:
        risk += 2
        print(f"  MEDIUM: {len(times)} timestamps reveal daily routine")
    
    level = 'CRITICAL' if risk >= 5 else 'HIGH' if risk >= 3 else 'LOW'
    print(f"\n  Overall privacy risk: {level} (score: {risk})")

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Forensic Image Analysis Pipeline
complete_examples.description: End-to-end: scan directory, identify, extract, analyse, report
complete_examples.code:
import os, hashlib, json, csv
from datetime import datetime

def forensic_image_pipeline(evidence_dir, output_dir):
    """Complete forensic image analysis pipeline."""
    os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
    
    # Phase 1: Discovery
    print("Phase 1: Discovering image files...")
    image_exts = {'.jpg', '.jpeg', '.png', '.gif', '.bmp', '.tiff', '.webp'}
    images = []
    
    for dirpath, _, filenames in os.walk(evidence_dir):
        for fname in filenames:
            fpath = os.path.join(dirpath, fname)
            ext = os.path.splitext(fname)[1].lower()
            if ext in image_exts:
                images.append(fpath)
    print(f"  Found {len(images)} image files")
    
    # Phase 2: Identification & Hashing
    print("Phase 2: Identifying and hashing...")
    results = []
    for fpath in images:
        with open(fpath, 'rb') as f:
            data = f.read()
        
        sha = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
        is_jpeg = data[:3] == b'\xff\xd8\xff'
        has_exif = b'Exif' in data[:500] if is_jpeg else False
        true_type = 'JPEG' if is_jpeg else 'PNG' if data[:4] == b'\x89PNG' else 'Other'
        
        ext = os.path.splitext(fpath)[1].lower()
        ext_match = (ext in ('.jpg', '.jpeg') and true_type == 'JPEG') or \
                    (ext == '.png' and true_type == 'PNG')
        
        results.append({
            'filename': os.path.basename(fpath),
            'path': fpath,
            'size': len(data),
            'sha256': sha,
            'true_type': true_type,
            'extension': ext,
            'ext_match': ext_match,
            'has_exif': has_exif,
        })
    
    # Phase 3: Analysis
    print("Phase 3: Analysing metadata patterns...")
    with_exif = sum(1 for r in results if r['has_exif'])
    mismatched = [r for r in results if not r['ext_match']]
    duplicates = []
    hashes = {}
    for r in results:
        if r['sha256'] in hashes:
            duplicates.append((r['filename'], hashes[r['sha256']]))
        else:
            hashes[r['sha256']] = r['filename']
    
    # Phase 4: Report Generation
    print("Phase 4: Generating reports...")
    
    # CSV manifest
    csv_path = os.path.join(output_dir, 'image_manifest.csv')
    with open(csv_path, 'w', newline='') as f:
        w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['filename', 'size', 'sha256', 'true_type', 'has_exif', 'ext_match'])
        w.writeheader()
        for r in results:
            w.writerow({k: r[k] for k in w.fieldnames})
    
    # Summary report
    summary = {
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'total_images': len(results),
        'with_exif': with_exif,
        'without_exif': len(results) - with_exif,
        'mismatched_types': len(mismatched),
        'duplicates': len(duplicates),
        'total_size': sum(r['size'] for r in results),
        'alerts': []
    }
    
    if mismatched:
        for m in mismatched:
            summary['alerts'].append(f"Type mismatch: {m['filename']} ({m['extension']} is actually {m['true_type']})")
    if duplicates:
        for d in duplicates:
            summary['alerts'].append(f"Duplicate: {d[0]} matches {d[1]}")
    
    json_path = os.path.join(output_dir, 'analysis.json')
    with open(json_path, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(summary, f, indent=2)
    
    # Print results
    print(f"\n{'=' * 55}")
    print(f"FORENSIC IMAGE ANALYSIS COMPLETE")
    print(f"{'=' * 55}")
    print(f"Images found:     {summary['total_images']}")
    print(f"With EXIF:        {summary['with_exif']}")
    print(f"Stripped/No EXIF: {summary['without_exif']}")
    print(f"Type mismatches:  {summary['mismatched_types']}")
    print(f"Duplicates:       {summary['duplicates']}")
    print(f"Total size:       {summary['total_size']:,} bytes")
    
    if summary['alerts']:
        print(f"\nALERTS ({len(summary['alerts'])}):")
        for alert in summary['alerts']:
            print(f"  >>> {alert}")
    
    print(f"\nReports: {csv_path}, {json_path}")
    return summary

# Create test evidence
os.makedirs('img_evidence', exist_ok=True)
test = [
    ('img_evidence/photo1.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + os.urandom(3000)),
    ('img_evidence/photo2.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe1\x00\x10Exif\x00\x00' + os.urandom(5000)),
    ('img_evidence/stripped.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0\x00\x10JFIF' + os.urandom(4000)),
    ('img_evidence/fake.jpg', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + os.urandom(2000)),
    ('img_evidence/screenshot.png', b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n' + os.urandom(1500)),
]
for path, content in test:
    with open(path, 'wb') as f: f.write(content)

forensic_image_pipeline('img_evidence', 'img_output')

import shutil
shutil.rmtree('img_evidence')
shutil.rmtree('img_output')

This is a COMPLETE forensic image pipeline:

Phase 1 (Discovery): os.walk finds all image files by extension.
Phase 2 (Identification): Magic bytes verify true type, SHA-256 hashes for integrity.
Phase 3 (Analysis): EXIF presence check, extension mismatch detection, duplicate finding.

Solution:
def fingerprint(make, model, serial=''):
    m = make.replace(' ', '_')
    mo = model.replace(' ', '_')
    sn = f'SN:{serial}' if serial else 'NO_SERIAL'
    return f'{m}|{mo}|{sn}'

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Distance Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Calculate distance between two GPS coordinates using Haversine formula.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Calculate distance between two GPS coordinates using Haversine formula.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import math

# TODO: Haversine distance function
def haversine(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):
    pass

# London to Paris

hints.message: Haversine: a = sin2(dlat/2) + cos(lat1)*cos(lat2)*sin2(dlon/2)
R = 6371 (Earth radius in km). Convert degrees to radians first!

Solution:
def haversine(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):
    R = 6371
    dlat = math.radians(lat2-lat1)
    dlon = math.radians(lon2-lon1)
    a = math.sin(dlat/2)**2 + math.cos(math.radians(lat1))*math.cos(math.radians(lat2))*math.sin(dlon/2)**2
    return R * 2 * math.asin(math.sqrt(a))

print(f'Distance: {haversine(51.5074, -0.1278, 48.8566, 2.3522):.1f} km')
